description: Archaeomagnetism is the study of the Earth's magnetic field as recorded in archaeological materials, used to date and understand the past. It involves analyzing the magnetic signatures in ferromagnetic materials to reconstruct the geomagnetic polarity time scale. This technique has been used to date sites within the last 10,000 years and has been instrumental in understanding the Earth's magnetic field and its variations over time.

nodes.label: Archaeomagnetism
nodes.description: The study of the Earth's magnetic field as recorded in archaeological materials
nodes.fun_fact: Archaeomagnetism was first conceived by Émile Thellier in the 1930s

nodes.label: Geomagnetic Reversals
nodes.description: The process by which the Earth's magnetic field reverses its polarity
nodes.fun_fact: Geomagnetic reversals have occurred many times in the Earth's history

nodes.label: Paleomagnetism
nodes.description: The study of the Earth's magnetic field as recorded in rocks and sediments
nodes.fun_fact: Paleomagnetism is used to reconstruct the Earth's magnetic field in the past

nodes.label: Magnetic Dating
nodes.description: The use of archaeomagnetism to date archaeological sites
nodes.fun_fact: Magnetic dating is used to date sites within the last 10,000 years

nodes.label: Geophysical Surveying
nodes.description: The use of geophysical techniques to survey and map the subsurface
nodes.fun_fact: Geophysical surveying is used to locate and map archaeological sites

nodes.label: Geoarchaeology
nodes.description: The application of geological techniques to archaeological research
nodes.fun_fact: Geoarchaeology is used to reconstruct the environmental and geological context of archaeological sites

nodes.label: Émile Thellier
nodes.description: The French geophysicist who first conceived the idea of archaeomagnetism
nodes.fun_fact: Thellier's work laid the foundation for the development of archaeomagnetism as a dating technique

nodes.label: SQUID Magnetometers
nodes.description: Highly sensitive magnetometers used to measure the magnetic properties of materials
nodes.fun_fact: SQUID magnetometers have greatly improved the accuracy and precision of archaeomagnetic dating

nodes.label: Italian Archaeomagnetic Data
nodes.description: A compilation of archaeomagnetic data from Italy, including new results and a preliminary secular variation curve
nodes.fun_fact: The Italian data set includes results from volcanic deposits of unquestionable age

nodes.label: Hungarian Archaeomagnetic Database
nodes.description: A database of archaeomagnetic directions from Hungary, with age estimates falling between BC 350 and AD 1800
nodes.fun_fact: The Hungarian database has been increased by the addition of 60 new directional results

nodes.label: British Directional Database
nodes.description: A database of archaeomagnetic directions from Britain, with age estimates falling between BC 2000 and AD 2000
nodes.fun_fact: The British database includes results from 466 archaeological sites

nodes.label: Radiometric Dating
nodes.description: A technique used to date materials by measuring the decay of radioactive isotopes
nodes.fun_fact: Radiometric dating is often used in conjunction with archaeomagnetism to date archaeological sites

nodes.label: Geomagnetic Polarity Time Scale
nodes.description: A record of the Earth's magnetic field polarity over time
nodes.fun_fact: The geomagnetic polarity time scale is used to constrain the age of materials

nodes.label: Ferromagnetic Materials
nodes.description: Materials that exhibit magnetic properties, such as magnetite
nodes.fun_fact: Ferromagnetic materials are used to record the Earth's magnetic field in archaeological materials

nodes.label: Curie Point
nodes.description: The temperature below which a material becomes ferromagnetic
nodes.fun_fact: The Curie point is important in archaeomagnetism as it determines when a material becomes magnetized

key_takeaways: Archaeomagnetism is a technique used to date archaeological sites by analyzing the magnetic properties of materials
Geomagnetic reversals and paleomagnetism are key concepts in archaeomagnetism
SQUID magnetometers have greatly improved the accuracy and precision of archaeomagnetic dating
real_world_impact: Archaeomagnetism has had a significant impact on our understanding of the past, allowing researchers to date archaeological sites with greater accuracy and precision. The technique has been used to date sites around the world, from the ancient civilizations of Italy and Hungary to the medieval sites of Britain. By reconstructing the Earth's magnetic field in the past, archaeomagnetism has also provided valuable insights into the Earth's geological and environmental history. As a result, archaeomagnetism has become an essential tool in the field of archaeology, enabling researchers to build a more detailed and accurate picture of human history.

images.title: Bernard Brunhes Memorial Plate.jpg
images.description: Plaque commemorating the centenary of the discovery by Bernard Brunhes of geomagnetic field reversals. This enamelled-lava plate is arranged at the entrance of the Observatoire de physique du globe de Clermont-Ferrand (OPGC), France.

images.title: Chibanian Strata Stake.jpg
images.description: The Chibanian stratum, which dates back to the Chiba period, is located along the Yoro River in Ichihara City, Chiba Prefecture.
